mission.title: Install and Configure Cluster API (CAPI)
mission.description: Cluster API (CAPI) is a Kubernetes operator that provides declarative APIs and tooling to simplify the provisioning, upgrading, and management of multiple Kubernetes clusters. It is particularly useful for platform operators who need to manage clusters across various infrastructure environments consistently. The trade-off is that it requires a deeper understanding of Kubernetes concepts and may involve a steeper learning curve for new users.

steps.title: Add the Cluster API Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Cluster API Helm repository to your local Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add cluster-api https://charts.cluster-api.sigs.k8s.io

```

> This repository contains the necessary charts for Cluster API.

steps.title: Install the Cluster API CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required for Cluster API to function.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-components.yaml

```

> This YAML file contains all the necessary CRDs.

steps.title: Install the Cluster API controller
steps.description:
This step installs the Cluster API controller using Helm, which manages the lifecycle of Kubernetes clusters.

```bash

helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version 1.12.4

```

> Make sure to install it in the kube-system namespace.

steps.title: Create a Cluster API custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates a custom resource that defines a new Kubernetes cluster managed by Cluster API.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  controlPlane:
    infrastructureRef:
      apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
      kind: MyInfrastructureCluster
      name: my-infrastructure
EOF

```

> Replace 'MyInfrastructureCluster' with your actual infrastructure provider.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  controlPlane:
    infrastructureRef:
      apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
      kind: MyInfrastructureCluster
      name: my-infrastructure
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Cluster API installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Cluster API components are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

kubectl get clusters

```

> You should see the Cluster API controller pods running.
> This will show the status of your created clusters.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Cluster API
uninstall.description:
This step removes Cluster API and its associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all managed clusters and their resources, leading to potential data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall cluster-api --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-components.yaml

```

> This command removes the Cluster API controller.
> This command removes the CRDs.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Cluster API
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your cluster configurations. This step will upgrade the Cluster API components to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get clusters -o yaml > backup-clusters.yaml

helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version 1.12.4

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> Backup your existing cluster configurations.
> Upgrade the Cluster API controller.
> Verify that the new pods are running.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no matches for kind "Cluster" in version "cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Cluster CRD is not installed or not recognized.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs using the command: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-components.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'failed to connect to the API server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually means that the kubeconfig is not set up correctly or the API server is down.

**Fix:**
Check your kubeconfig file and ensure the API server is running: kubectl cluster-info

troubleshooting.title: Pods in kube-system are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can occur if there are misconfigurations in the Cluster API components.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the failing pods for errors: kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kube-system

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no resources found in kube-system namespace'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Cluster API components have not been installed correctly.

**Fix:**
Reinstall the Cluster API components using the Helm install command.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new infrastructure providers and improved the stability of existing controllers.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the old API version 'v1beta1' for Cluster resources.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.11.x, ensure to update your Cluster CRD definitions to the new API version.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get clusters' showing your clusters with their statuses, and 'kubectl get pods -n kube-system' showing all Cluster API components running without errors.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  controlPlane:
    infrastructureRef:
      apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
      kind: MyInfrastructureCluster
      name: my-infrastructure
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.26 or higher and have kubectl and Helm installed.
